coredata.dc:title: A new di-recognition and di-functional nanosurface aptamer molecularly imprinted polymer probe for trace glyphosate with SERS/RRS/Abs trimode technique
coredata.dc:creator: He, Xue
He, Yingying
Li, Chongning
Jiang, Zhiliang
coredata.dc:description: A new di-recognition nitrogen-doped carbon dot nanosurface aptamer molecularly imprinted polymer (CDNAg@MIPApt) nanocatalytic di-functional probe was prepared by microwave irradiation. The probe was utilized nitrogen-doped silver carbon dots (CDNAg) as the matrix, glyphosate (Gly) as the template molecule, α-methyl acrylate as the monomer, ethylene glycol dimethacrylate as the cross-linker, and aptamer as the biorecognition element. It could not only recognize Gly but also exhibits catalytic amplification function. It was found that CDNAg@MIPApt catalyzed the redox reaction of polyethylene glycol 400 (PEG400)-AgNO3 to generate silver nanoparticles (AgNPs). The AgNPs indicator component exhibit the effects of surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS), resonance Rayleigh scattering (RRS) and surface plasmon resonance absorption (Abs). In the presence of Gly, it binds to the surface imprinted site of CDNAg@MIPApt, to reduce AgNPs generation due to the catalytic activity of CDNAg@MIPApt decreasing. Thus, the SERS/RRS/Abs signal values decreased linearly. The linear ranges of SERS/RRS/Abs assay were 0.1–2.5 nM, 0.25–2.75 nM and 0.5–5 nM respectively. The detection limits were 0.034 nM, 0.071 nM and 0.18 nM Gly.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Glyphosate
Naosurface molecularly imprinted polymer
Aptamer
Nanocatalytic amplification
SERS
RRS

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and energy spectral analysis (EDS)
RRS spectra of catalytic analysis systems
SERS spectra of catalytic analysis systems
Abs spectra of catalytic recognition systems

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Scientific Research Foundation of Guangxi Education Department (No. 2022KY0509), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22266013, 22264005) and the Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi (No. 2022GXNSFDA035073).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme of the SERS/RRS/Abs determination of Gly based on nanosurface molecularly imprinted polymer nanoprobe catalytic amplification and recognition (A), and CDNAg@MIPApt nanocatalytic reaction mechanism (B).
A: TEM map of CDNAg; B–C: TEM map of MIP; D: TEM of CDNAg@MIP; E-F: TEM of CDNAg@MIPApt; G-K: Mapping map of CDNAg@MIPApt; L-M: EDS of MIP, CDNAg@MIPApt.

A: MIP-PEG400-AgNO3-Gly, (0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 30, 40) nM Gly+0.75 mM AgNO3+21.4 mg/mL PEG400 + 6.86 mM NaAc-HAc (pH = 4.6)+50 mg/L CDNAg@MIP; B: CDNAg@MIP-PEG400-AgNO3-Gly, (0, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50) nM Gly+ 0.75 mM AgNO3+21.4 mg/mL PEG400 + 6.86 mM NaAc-HAc(pH = 4.6)+50 mg/L CDNAg@MIP; C: CDNAg@MIPApt-PEG400-AgNO3-Gly, (0. 0.25, 0.75, 1.25, 1.75, 2.25, 2.75) nM Gly+0.75 mM AgNO3+21.4 mg/mL PEG400 + 6.86 mM NaAc-HAc(pH = 4.6)+2.5 mg/L CDNAg@MIPApt. D: MIP-PEG400-AgNO3-Gly，a–g: 0, 2.5, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25 nM Gly+0.75 mM AgNO3+21.4 mg/mL PEG400 + 6.86 mM NaAc-HAc(pH = 4.6)+3.75 mg/L CDNAg@MIP+0.5 μmol/L VB4r+25 mmol/L NaCl;E: CDNAg@MIP-PEG400-AgNO3-Gly, a–g: 0, 2.5, 7.5, 12.5, 17.5, 22.5, 27.5 nM Gly+0.75 mM AgNO3+21.4 mg/mL PEG400 + 6.86 mM NaAc-HAc (pH = 4.6)+3.75 mg/L CDNAg@MIP+0.5 μmol/L VB4r+25 mmol/L NaCl;F: CDNAg@MIPApt-PEG400-AgNO3-Gly, a–g: 0, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 1.5, 2, 2.5 nM Gly+0.75 mM AgNO3+21.4 mg/mL PEG400 + 6.86 mM NaAc-HAc (pH = 4.6)+5.75 mg/L CDNAg@MIPApt+0.5 μmol/L VB4r+25 mmol/L NaCl. G: CDNAg@MIP-PEG400-AgNO3-Gly, a–g: 0, 1, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 3.5 nM Gly+ 0.75 mM AgNO3+ 21.4 mg/mL PEG400 + 6.86 mM NaAc-Hc (pH = 4.6)+3.75 mg/L CDNAg@MIPApt. H: CDNAg@MIPApt-PEG400-AgNO3-Gly, a–g: 0, 0.5, 0.75, 1, 1.25, 1.5, 1.75 nM Gly+0.75 mM AgNO3+21.4 mg/mL PEG400 + 6.86 mM NaAc-HAc(pH = 4.6)+3.75 mg/L CDNAg@MIPApt.

head.ce:title: A new di-recognition and di-functional nanosurface aptamer molecularly imprinted polymer probe for trace glyphosate with SERS/RRS/Abs trimode technique

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Public Health, Guilin Medical University, Guilin, 541199, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Public Health, Guilin Medical University, Guilin, 541199, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Environmental Pollution Control Theory and Technology, Guilin, 541006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Environmental Pollution Control Theory and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Environmental Pollution Control Theory and Technology, Guilin, 541006, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Public Health, Guilin Medical University, Guilin, 541199, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new di-recognition nitrogen-doped carbon dot nanosurface aptamer molecularly imprinted polymer (CDNAg@MIPApt) nanocatalytic di-functional probe was prepared by microwave irradiation. The probe was utilized nitrogen-doped silver carbon dots (CDNAg) as the matrix, glyphosate (Gly) as the template molecule, α-methyl acrylate as the monomer, ethylene glycol dimethacrylate as the cross-linker, and aptamer as the biorecognition element. It could not only recognize Gly but also exhibits catalytic amplification function. It was found that CDNAg@MIPApt catalyzed the redox reaction of polyethylene glycol 400 (PEG400)-AgNO3 to generate silver nanoparticles (AgNPs). The AgNPs indicator component exhibit the effects of surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS), resonance Rayleigh scattering (RRS) and surface plasmon resonance absorption (Abs). In the presence of Gly, it binds to the surface imprinted site of CDNAg@MIPApt, to reduce AgNPs generation due to the catalytic activity of CDNAg@MIPApt decreasing. Thus, the SERS/RRS/Abs signal values decreased linearly. The linear ranges of SERS/RRS/Abs assay were 0.1–2.5 nM, 0.25–2.75 nM and 0.5–5 nM respectively. The detection limits were 0.034 nM, 0.071 nM and 0.18 nM Gly.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) are synthetic polymers with recognition ability, structural predictability, high affinity and low cost (Chen et al., 2021; Ertürk and Mattiasson, 2017; Liu and Dykstra, 2022). In recent years, MIPs have been applied to sensors (Ahmad et al., 2019; Çimen et al., 2018), chromatography (Boysen, 2019; Helali et al., 2024; Zhao et al., 2023) and polymerization (Miao et al., 2023). Detection platforms based on MIPs often have the characteristics of high selectivity, stability and reusability. They have been applied to the rapid detection of various substances (Keçili et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2023). Shi (Shi et al., 2023) created a surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) and resonance Rayleigh Scattering (RRS) dual-mode sensor using the nanogold indicator reaction and a recognition element MIP for detection of ultra-trace acetamidine. However, MIPs suffer from some defects in practical applications, such as embedding too deep cavities, uneven distribution of binding sites, and slow recombination kinetics. Core@shell particles are a powerful means to solve the above defects. It is usually a material core covered by MIP, and the commonly used core materials are solid single-core materials, solid composites, and hollow core materials. The shell structure on the surface provides uniformly distributed binding sites, which greatly improves the binding kinetics (Bhogal et al., 2020). At the same time, core materials with different functions add new functionalities to MIPs with Core@shell particles (Ruiz-Córdova et al., 2021). Feng et al. (2022) prepared AuNPs@MIP nanoparticles as SERS substrates for detection of 2,4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid (2,4-D) by wrapping an ultrathin MIP shell layer on the surface of AuNPs, with a SERS detection limit of 0.011 μg/mL 2,4-D. Aptamer (Apt) is an excellent recognition element, and it has a greater advantage than antibodies. It has been used in assays of mycotoxins (Chen et al., 2020a), pathogens (Chen et al., 2020b) and metal pollutants (B. Wang et al., 2023). The dual recognition MIPs-Apt system was combined Apt and MIPs into a hybrid system for the detection of the target analyte, with both properties. It is not only increase sensitivity but also improve selectivity (Ali and Omer, 2022; Bai et al., 2013; Li et al., 2019; Mahmoud et al., 2020; Roushani et al., 2022). Roushani et al. (2022) designed an electrochemical biosensor for aflatoxin B1 by mixing MIPs and Apt on a Cu-modified glassy carbon electrode with a detection limit of 12.0 pg/L.

SERS is a non-elastic scattering molecular spectrum (Li et al., 2021). It was applied in many fields such as environment (Hao et al., 2015), food (Yang et al., 2022) and others. Tian et al. (2022) developed a SERS Apt sensor for detection of severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus type 2. Yang et al. (2022) developed a SERS method for benzoic acid, using Ag- covalent-organic framework as the SERS substrate. RRS is an elastic scattering, with simplicity, sensitivity, and has been applied to trace metal ions and pesticides (Liang et al., 2022; Lv et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2022; Li et al., 2023) and cells analysis (Junbo Li et al., 2023). The di-scattering method is of the two advantages, and it is significant to combining di-scattering with the di-recognition for assay of Glyphosate (Gly).

Gly is an organophosphorus compound and the most widely used herbicide. International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC) classified Gly as a “probable carcinogen” in humans. It is primarily cytotoxic and genotoxic (Peillex and Pelletier, 2020). Therefore, simple, rapid and sensitive detection of Gly is necessary. Currently the methods include immunoassay (Viirlaid et al., 2019), colorimetric assay (Aydin and Keleş, 2022), capillary electrophoresis-mass spectrometry (Graf et al., 2022). These methods require skillful operation and complicated pretreatment, and the sensitivity and selectivity are not ideal. Recently, some new methods have emerged, Mikac et al. (2022) used SERS to detect 100 μM Gly. As far as we know, there are no reports on the use of di-recognition MIPApt polymers for constructing SERS/RRS/Abs trimode biosensors for quantitative analysis of Gly. This study uniquely prepared a di-recognition (Apt and MIP) and di-functional (recognition and catalytic amplification) nanoprobe. It not only improved the sensitivity but also increased the selectivity. A new CDNAg@MIPApt catalytic nanosilver indication reaction was combined with the di-recognition probe to develop a facile, sensitive and selective SERS/RRS/Abs trimode method.
More information is provided in the supporting materials (SM).

Under the microwave irradiation, Core@shell particles CDNAg@MIPApt nanoprobes with catalytic activity and specific recognition were synthesized. There are both Apt-Gly conjugate and Gly on the nanoprobe, and both can be imprinted on the nanosurface to form specific recognition sites (Ghanbari and Roushani, 2018). The PEG400 and AgNO3 can adsorb on the surface, and produced slowly AgNPs. CDNAg has a good catalytic effect on the redox reaction to generate more AgNPs with a strong RRS and Abs peak at 450 nm, and a strong SERS peak at 1615 cm−1 after the addition of NaCl and VB4r probe molecules. After CDNAg was wrapped by MIP, it formed a Core@shell particles CDNAg@MIPApt nanoprobes with Apt. When Gly was added, the recognition site on the surface bound to Gly, forming a CDNAg@MIPApt-Gly complex. The catalysis decreases, AgNPs generation decreases, and the signal decreases (Majdinasab et al., 2021). Accordingly, a SERS/RRS/Abs three-mode sensing analysis platform with high sensitivity and specific detection of Gly was established (Fig. 1 A).

Apt reacts with target molecule to form specific three-dimensional structures by intermolecular interactions such as base or functional group pairing (Seok Kim et al., 2016). This process does not involve chemical bonding. The complex of aptamer-target molecule can fold tightly into the nanosurface MIP (CDNAg@MIPApt) by means of the three-dimensional structures, and it is not easy to wash off from the surface according to the reference (Tan et al., 2018). In addition, the nanocatalytic mechanism was studied (Fig. 1B) as in the SM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Molecular spectroscopy, fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray diffraction (XRD)
ce:section.ce:para: The Flu/RRS/Abs spectra of CDNAg/MIP/CDNAg@MIPApt show in Fig. S1. CDNAg exhibits a fluorescence peak at 510 nm with excitation wavelength of 370 nm (Fig. S1A), a RRS peak at 500 nm (Fig. S1B), and an Abs peak at 340 nm (Fig. S1C). The emission spectra of CDNAg@MIPApt were recorded (Fig. S1D), with excitation wavelengths of 250, 275, 300, 325, 350, 375, 400, and 425 nm at 300 V. The CDNAg@MIPApt had no fluorescence due to CD completely wrapped by MIP. MIP and CD@MIPApt had RRS (Figs. S1E and S1F) and Abs peaks (Figs. S1G and S1H) at 380 nm and 230 nm.

The CDNAg/MIP/CDNAg@MIP/CDNAg@MIPApt FT-IR spectra were recorded (Fig. S1I). The peaks of CDNAg at 3348 cm−1 were triggered by O–H and N–H stretching, the peak at 1633 cm−1 was caused by CO/CN backbone vibration, and the peak at 1400 cm−1 was due to the bending vibration of C–N. The peaks of MIP, CDNAg@MIP, and CDNAg@MIPApt at 2960 cm−1 were originated from C–H stretching, and the peak at 1723 cm−1 is due to CO/CN stretching in MAA and EGDMA. The peak at 1454 cm−1 is due to CH2 variable angle vibration, and the peak at 1159 cm−1 originates from C–O–C stretching (Zhang et al., 2022).

XRD patterns were obtained in the range of 2θ = 5–60° (Fig. S1J). MIP, CDNAg@MIP, and CDNAg@MIPApt all have a peak packet at 2θ = 10–25° showing an amorphous carbon structure, suggesting that all three have a similar amorphous structure. CDNAg@MIP and CDNAg@MIPApt have a peak near 2θ = 37.88°, showing the presence of Ag (100), which is consistent with the fact that the nanomaterial is nucleated by CD doped with N and Ag (Ragavan and Pandurangan, 2022).

XPS spectrograms revealed the elemental composition of CDNAg/MIP/CDNAg@MIP/CDNAg@MIPApt. In the full-scan measurement spectra of CDNAg (Fig. S2A), the high-resolution C1s spectrogram has three peaks of CC, C–O/C–N, and CO (Fig. S2B). The O1s has two peaks of C–O and CO (Fig. S2C), and three peaks can be observed in the N1s spectrogram (Fig. S2D), which corresponds to the three morphologies of pyridinium nitrogen, pyrrolyl nitrogen, and graphitic nitrogen, respectively (Shi et al., 2022). The high-resolution C1s spectrogram of MIP/CDNAg@MIPApt was further back-convoluted into three peaks corresponding to CC, C–O/C–N, and CO (Fig. S2E). The high-resolution spectrogram of O1s of MIP had three peaks, C–O, CO, and O–H, while only two peaks, C–O and CO, were observed for CDNAg@MIPApt (Fig. S2F). MIP/CDNAg@MIPApt high-resolution N1s deconvolution spectrogram has one peak for C–N (Fig. S2G) (Ragavan and Pandurangan, 2022; Zhang et al., 2022).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and energy spectral analysis (EDS)
ce:section.ce:para: The CDNAg, MIP, CDNAg@MIP and CDNAg@MIPApt were scanned by TEM and their morphology was recorded. The size of CDNAg (Fig. 2 A) was about 1–5 nm with round shape. MIP (Fig. 2B and C), CDNAg@MIP (Fig. 2D) and CDNAg@MIPApt (Fig. 2E and F) were spherical particles, with larger particles and a layer of transparent aperture at the edge. The mapping diagrams of CDNAg@MIPApt (Fig. 2G–K) and the corresponding EDS (Fig. 2L, M) were obtained, both of them are mainly composed of the C, N and O elements. CDNAg@MIPApt is doped with a small amount of Ag, and Ag is uniformly distributed in CDNAg@MIPApt.
The specific surface (Fig. S3), Zeta potential and particle size analysis (Fig. S4), and stability (Fig. S5) are provided in the SM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: RRS spectra of catalytic analysis systems
ce:section.ce:para: The RRS spectra of catalytically amplified systems (Fig. S6) are provided in the Supporting Information. When the target Gly was added in the analytical system, it could bind to the specific site and inhibited its catalysis. The system generated fewer AgNPs and lower RRS signal values. The CDNAg@MIPApt probe has a strongest ability to recognize, capture, and bind Gly. Its RRS peak at 450 nm gradually decreased with the increase of Gly concentration and showed a linear relationship. The most sensitive CDNAg@MIPApt system was chosen for use (Fig. 3 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: SERS spectra of catalytic analysis systems
ce:section.ce:para: The SERS spectra (Steffi Antony and Shankar Hyam, 2023) of catalytic amplification systems (Fig. S7) are provided in the SM. After the addition of Gly to the MIP-PEG400-AgNO3 system, the catalysis was inhibited. With the increase of the Gly concentration, the AgNPs reduced, and the SERS signals at 1615 cm−1 decreased. Comparison of the MIP and CDNAg@MIP, the CDNAg@MIPApt system (Fig. 3D–F) had the largest slope and the lowest detection limit.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Abs spectra of catalytic recognition systems
ce:section.ce:para: The AgNPs were yellow color with a surface plasmon resonance absorption peak at 450 nm. For the PEG400-AgNO3-Gly recognition system, the catalytic activity of CDNAg@MIP, CDNAg@MIPApt gradually weakened with the increase of Gly concentration. The AgNPs gradually decreased, and the solution slowly changed from yellow to colorless. The absorption spectra show that the absorbance change at 450 nm was linearly related to Gly concentration (Fig. 3G、3H). The result is in agreement with SERS and RRS spectra, but the Abs signal is smaller and less sensitive than the scattering method.
More information (Table S1) is provided in the SM.
More information (Figs. S8–S11) is provided in the SM.

Under the conditions, the concentration of Gly showed a good linear relationship with the SERS/RRS/Abs signal values. Among them, the SERS system was more sensitive, and the regression equation was ΔI1615cm-1 = 2632.9CGly + 332.4, with a correlation coefficient of 0.9948, and a linear range (LR) of 0.1–2.5 nM. The standard deviation Sd was obtained from 11 parallel determinations of the blank specimen, and the detection limit (DL) was calculated according to the equations DL = 3 × Sd/N (N is the slope of the working curve). It was found that the DL of CDNAg@MIPAPt specific identification was lowest (Table S2). Compared with the reported Gly assay (Table S3) (Canseco Caballero et al., 2023; Q. Li et al., 2023; Q. Wang et al., 2023; Zhang et al., 2023), this method has high sensitivity.
More information (Table S4) is provided in the SM.

Laboratory tap water, grass water, irrigation water and field water were collected. The samples were placed in 50 mL centrifuge tubes, numbered as A, B, C, D, E, F, G, H, and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10min, and the supernatant was filtered through filter paper to remove the larger particles and impurities in the water and then prepared for use (Yu et al., 2021). The water samples were taken 1.0 mL, and the Gly content was determined according to the procedure. Each sample was measured in parallel for 5 times, and its relative standard deviation (RSD) was calculated. A known concentration of Gly was added to the samples and determined, and the recovery was calculated. The RSD was 1.83–4.52% and the recovery was 95.7–102% (Table S5).

A new di-recognition and di-functional nanosurface molecularly imprinted polymer (CDNAg@MIPApt) was synthesized successfully by microwave irradiation, utilizing glyphosate as the template, aptamers for biorecognition, and carbon dots as the nanosubstrate. Characterization was confirmed the successful integration of the components. CDNAg@MIPApt showed dual functionalities, including glyphosate recognition and nano-catalytic amplification, the generated silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) are of SERS/RRS activity. Leveraging both imprinting and aptamer interactions, a selective, sensitive and efficient tri-modal SERS/RRS/Abs assay was established for trace glyphosate detection. In the future work, we will explore highly catalysis nanoenzyme and strongly recognition element, and it was organically coupled with MIP to improve the sensitivity and selectivity, and applly to analysis of Gly in complex matrices environmental or food samples.

Xue He: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Software, Data curation. Yingying He: Writing – original draft, Software, Formal analysis. Chongning Li: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Zhiliang Jiang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: Authors declare that they have no financial or personal conflicts of interest related to this research. We guarantee that this research is original and has not been published or submitted for publication elsewhere.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Scientific Research Foundation of Guangxi Education Department (No. 2022KY0509), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22266013, 22264005) and the Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi (No. 2022GXNSFDA035073).
